out_of_scope: practices from other AP science courses not aligned to AP Physics 1
inquiry skills assessed only in lab portfolio
classification_hints: question asks to sketch, plot, derive, justify, verify, or design an experiment
common_misconceptions: science practices are separate from content and can be ignored

scope: 1.A: create diagrams or models of physical situations
1.B: create graphs or charts representing data or relationships
out_of_scope: creating representations that require calculus-based derivations
classification_hints: question asks to draw, sketch, plot, label, or represent a physical situation
common_misconceptions: any drawing is a representation; the representation must connect to a physics model

common_misclassifications.reason: both may involve equations, but 1.C focuses on representation while 2.A/B/C focus on mathematical routines

scope: 2.A: justify a claim using qualitative and quantitative reasoning
2.D: predict new values using functional dependence
classification_hints: question asks to calculate, compare, estimate, justify quantitatively, or predict a value
common_misconceptions: mathematical routines are only about getting a number; they include reasoning and justification

common_misclassifications.reason: both may involve claims; 2.A/B/C/D focus on quantitative justification, 3.A/B/C focus on argumentation and experimental reasoning

scope: 3.B: identify patterns or trends in data
out_of_scope: designing experiments beyond the scope of AP Physics 1 content
arguments requiring content outside the 8 units
classification_hints: question asks to design, describe a procedure, identify a trend, or justify a claim with evidence
common_misconceptions: argumentation is just opinion; it must use physics evidence and reasoning

common_misclassifications.reason: both may justify claims; 3.C focuses on evidence-based argumentation while 2.A focuses on quantitative reasoning

scope: scalars and vectors in one dimension
vectors and motion in two dimensions
classification_hints: question involves position, velocity, acceleration, motion graphs, projectiles, or relative motion without forces
common_misconceptions: velocity and acceleration are always in the same direction
zero velocity means zero acceleration

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve motion; kinematics describes motion without reference to forces

topics.topic_name: Scalars and Vectors in One Dimension
scope: add and subtract vectors in one dimension
out_of_scope: unit vector notation beyond one dimension
classification_hints: question asks about magnitude, direction, or sign of a one-dimensional quantity
common_misconceptions: negative sign always means smaller magnitude

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve vectors; 1.1 stays in one dimension

scope: definitions and relationships between displacement, velocity, and acceleration
out_of_scope: derivation of kinematic equations using calculus
classification_hints: question uses kinematic equations or asks for displacement/velocity/acceleration relationships
common_misconceptions: acceleration is the same as velocity
deceleration always means negative acceleration

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve kinematic quantities; 1.2 focuses on algebraic relationships while 1.3 focuses on graphical representations

scope: translating between verbal, graphical, and algebraic descriptions
out_of_scope: motion maps not used in AP Physics 1
classification_hints: question provides or asks for a motion graph and interprets slope or area
common_misconceptions: slope of position-time graph gives acceleration
area under velocity-time graph gives position without sign consideration

common_misclassifications.reason: both use kinematic quantities; 1.3 emphasizes graphical representation

scope: describe motion relative to different observers
classification_hints: question compares observations from two different frames of reference
common_misconceptions: absolute rest exists; motion is always relative to a chosen frame

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve velocity; 1.4 focuses on observer dependence

topics.topic_name: Vectors and Motion in Two Dimensions
scope: vector addition and subtraction in two dimensions
classification_hints: question involves vector components, projectiles, or circular motion at constant speed
common_misconceptions: horizontal and vertical motions of a projectile are not independent
centripetal acceleration is outward

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve circular motion; 1.5 treats kinematics of uniform circular motion while 2.9 treats dynamics

out_of_scope: friction on inclined planes beyond algebraic treatment
classification_hints: question involves forces, Newton's laws, free-body diagrams, or dynamics of objects
common_misconceptions: force is required to maintain motion
action-reaction pairs act on the same object

common_misclassifications.reason: both describe motion; Unit 2 explains motion using forces

scope: center of mass of a system
out_of_scope: calculating center of mass by integration for irregular objects
extended rigid body rotation in this topic
classification_hints: question asks to choose or analyze a system boundary or center-of-mass motion
common_misconceptions: center of mass must lie inside the object

common_misclassifications.reason: both may involve system motion; 2.1 focuses on defining the system and center of mass

out_of_scope: forces not covered in AP Physics 1 such as electric or magnetic forces
classification_hints: question asks to identify forces or draw a free-body diagram
common_misconceptions: normal force always equals weight
velocity direction indicates net force direction

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve forces; 2.2 focuses on identification and representation

out_of_scope: third-law applications involving fields outside mechanics
classification_hints: question asks about forces between two interacting objects
common_misconceptions: action and reaction cancel each other on the same object

common_misclassifications.reason: both use Newton's laws; 2.3 focuses on paired forces

scope: objects at rest or moving with constant velocity
out_of_scope: rotational equilibrium, which is covered in Unit 5
classification_hints: question involves zero net force and constant velocity or rest
common_misconceptions: an object at rest has no forces acting on it

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve net force; 2.4 is the special case of zero net force

classification_hints: question asks to relate net force to acceleration or solve dynamics problems
common_misconceptions: mass and weight are the same quantity
larger force always means larger velocity

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve net force; 2.5 handles non-zero net force

out_of_scope: gravitational potential energy beyond mgh near Earth
classification_hints: question involves weight or gravitational force near Earth's surface
common_misconceptions: weight is the same on the Moon as on Earth
mass changes with location

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve gravitational effects; 2.6 focuses on force, 3.3 on gravitational potential energy

out_of_scope: friction coefficients on complex curved surfaces
classification_hints: question involves surfaces in contact resisting motion or impending motion
common_misconceptions: static friction always equals mu_s N
friction always opposes motion

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve net force; 2.7 specifically addresses contact friction

out_of_scope: series/parallel spring combinations beyond simple algebraic treatment
classification_hints: question involves a spring and Hooke's law
common_misconceptions: spring force is constant regardless of displacement

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve springs; 2.8 treats spring as a force in dynamics, 7 treats spring as an oscillator

classification_hints: question asks for the force causing circular motion or relates tension/gravity/friction to circular path
common_misconceptions: centripetal force is a new separate force
outward centrifugal force exists in inertial frame

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve circular motion; 2.9 focuses on forces causing circular motion

out_of_scope: energy in fields beyond gravitational and spring
classification_hints: question involves energy, work, power, or conservation of energy
common_misconceptions: work is force times distance without considering displacement in force direction
potential energy is a property of an object alone

common_misclassifications.reason: both may involve forces; Unit 3 focuses on energy transfer and storage

scope: kinetic energy of a point mass or system
out_of_scope: kinetic energy of rotation, which is Unit 6
classification_hints: question asks for kinetic energy due to translational motion
common_misconceptions: kinetic energy is proportional to velocity rather than velocity squared

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve kinetic energy; 3.1 is translational, 6.1 is rotational

scope: work done by a constant force
out_of_scope: work done by variable forces requiring integration
classification_hints: question asks to calculate work or relate work to energy change
common_misconceptions: work is done whenever a force acts, regardless of displacement

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve energy storage/transfer; 3.2 is work as transfer, 3.3 is potential energy as stored

out_of_scope: potential energy in electric or magnetic fields
classification_hints: question involves stored energy due to position or spring deformation
common_misconceptions: potential energy is absolute rather than dependent on reference level

common_misclassifications.reason: both are forms of mechanical energy; 3.3 is stored, 3.1 is due to motion

classification_hints: question tracks energy forms through a process or uses energy conservation
common_misconceptions: mechanical energy is always conserved regardless of friction

common_misclassifications.reason: both are conservation laws; 3.4 conserves energy, 4.3 conserves linear momentum

scope: power as rate of energy transfer
classification_hints: question asks for rate of work or energy transfer
common_misconceptions: power is the same as energy or work

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve work; 3.5 focuses on rate

out_of_scope: momentum in two dimensions beyond vector component treatment
classification_hints: question involves collisions, impulse, or momentum conservation
common_misconceptions: momentum and kinetic energy are always conserved together
impulse is force times time without vector direction

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve conserved quantities; momentum is vector and relates to forces over time

classification_hints: question asks for momentum of an object or system
common_misconceptions: momentum is a scalar

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve mass and velocity; momentum is vector and relates to collisions

classification_hints: question relates force applied over time to momentum change
common_misconceptions: impulse is the same as work

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve force and displacement/time; impulse is force times time

scope: conservation of momentum in collisions and explosions
out_of_scope: momentum conservation in multi-dimensional collisions beyond component treatment
classification_hints: question uses conservation of momentum in an isolated system
common_misconceptions: momentum is conserved for individual objects rather than systems

common_misclassifications.reason: both are conservation laws; momentum conservation applies to isolated systems

scope: inelastic collisions do not conserve kinetic energy
out_of_scope: two-dimensional collision analysis beyond component treatment
classification_hints: question classifies a collision or compares kinetic energy before and after
common_misconceptions: all collisions conserve kinetic energy
inelastic means no momentum conservation

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve collisions; 4.4 focuses on kinetic energy conservation distinction

classification_hints: question involves rotation about a fixed axis, torque, or rotational inertia
common_misconceptions: torque is force
rotational inertia depends only on mass

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve forces and motion; Unit 5 focuses on rotation

scope: rotational kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration
classification_hints: question describes rotation with angular quantities
common_misconceptions: angular acceleration and tangential acceleration are the same

common_misclassifications.reason: both use kinematic equations; 5.1 is for rotation

scope: arc length, tangential velocity, tangential acceleration
out_of_scope: vector cross products for velocity and acceleration
classification_hints: question relates angular quantities to linear quantities for a rotating object
common_misconceptions: all points on a rotating object have the same linear speed

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve circular motion; 5.2 links angular and linear descriptions

scope: torque from a force about a pivot
classification_hints: question asks for torque or its effect on rotation
common_misconceptions: torque depends only on force magnitude

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve forces causing acceleration; 5.3 causes angular acceleration

scope: rotational inertia of point masses and common shapes
dependence on mass distribution and axis
out_of_scope: integration to find rotational inertia of irregular shapes
classification_hints: question involves resistance to angular acceleration or given I values
common_misconceptions: rotational inertia is the same for all axes through an object

common_misclassifications.reason: both affect rotational acceleration; 5.4 is the resistance property

topics.topic_name: Rotational Equilibrium and Newton's First Law in Rotational Form
classification_hints: question involves balancing torques or objects in rotational equilibrium
common_misconceptions: zero net force guarantees zero net torque

common_misclassifications.reason: both are equilibrium conditions; 5.5 adds torque balance

topics.topic_name: Newton's Second Law in Rotational Form
out_of_scope: systems with time-varying rotational inertia requiring calculus
classification_hints: question relates net torque to angular acceleration
common_misconceptions: larger torque always means larger angular velocity

common_misclassifications.reason: both are Newton's second law; 5.6 is the rotational form

topics.topic_name: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems
classification_hints: question involves energy, work, or momentum in rotating systems
common_misconceptions: angular momentum is a scalar
rolling objects have only translational kinetic energy

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve rotation; Unit 6 focuses on energy and momentum of rotating systems

scope: total kinetic energy of rolling objects
classification_hints: question asks for kinetic energy of a rotating or rolling object
common_misconceptions: rotational kinetic energy is independent of axis

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve kinetic energy; 6.1 is rotational

classification_hints: question relates torque through an angle to rotational kinetic energy change
common_misconceptions: work by torque is force times distance only

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve work; 6.2 is work by torque

scope: angular momentum of a point mass or rigid object
out_of_scope: angular momentum in three dimensions using cross products
classification_hints: question involves angular momentum or torque applied over time
common_misconceptions: angular momentum depends only on angular velocity

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve impulse; 6.3 is angular

scope: conservation of angular momentum in isolated systems
examples such as spinning figure skater
out_of_scope: systems with external torque not along axis
classification_hints: question uses conservation of angular momentum when external torque is zero
common_misconceptions: angular momentum is conserved whenever energy is conserved

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve angular momentum; 6.4 is the conservation law

scope: relationship between linear and angular speed
out_of_scope: rolling with slipping requiring advanced friction analysis
rolling on curved surfaces requiring calculus
classification_hints: question involves a rolling object and the no-slip condition
common_misconceptions: rolling objects have only translational kinetic energy

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve rotational kinetic energy; 6.5 specifically addresses rolling motion

classification_hints: question treats a satellite in circular orbit using gravitational force
common_misconceptions: satellites in orbit are weightless because there is no gravity

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve centripetal force; 6.6 applies to orbital motion

out_of_scope: pendulum on other planets beyond algebraic treatment
classification_hints: question involves oscillating systems, springs, or simple pendulums
common_misconceptions: period of a simple pendulum depends on mass
amplitude affects period of a mass-spring system

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve springs; 7 treats oscillatory motion

classification_hints: question identifies or explains the condition for SHM
common_misconceptions: any back-and-forth motion is SHM

common_misclassifications.reason: both describe SHM; 7.1 defines the condition, 7.2 quantifies period/frequency

scope: period and frequency of mass-spring system
classification_hints: question asks for period or frequency of an oscillator
common_misconceptions: period of pendulum depends on amplitude for small angles
mass does not affect mass-spring period

common_misclassifications.reason: both analyze SHM; 7.2 focuses on period/frequency

scope: graphs of displacement, velocity, and acceleration
classification_hints: question provides or asks for graphs of SHM quantities
common_misconceptions: velocity is maximum at maximum displacement

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve graphs; 7.3 is specific to oscillations

scope: exchange between kinetic and potential energy
classification_hints: question tracks energy forms in an oscillator
common_misconceptions: total energy in SHM varies with position

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve conservation of mechanical energy; 7.4 is in oscillators

classification_hints: question involves liquids, gases, pressure, buoyancy, or fluid flow
common_misconceptions: pressure acts only downward
objects float because they are light

common_misclassifications.reason: both may involve forces; Unit 8 applies Newton's laws to fluids

scope: mass density and particle model of fluids
classification_hints: question involves density or particle spacing in fluids
common_misconceptions: density is the same as weight

common_misclassifications.reason: both describe fluid properties; 8.1 focuses on density

out_of_scope: pressure in moving fluids beyond qualitative treatment
absolute pressure calculations in complex geometries
classification_hints: question asks about pressure at a depth or pressure differences
common_misconceptions: pressure in a fluid pushes only downward

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve forces in fluids; 8.2 focuses on pressure as a scalar property

out_of_scope: derivation of buoyant force using surface integrals
classification_hints: question involves buoyancy or forces on submerged objects
common_misconceptions: buoyant force depends on depth of submersion
floating objects experience no buoyant force

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve gravitational force; 8.3 addresses buoyancy in fluids

scope: conservation of mass and energy in fluid flow
classification_hints: question relates flow speed to cross-sectional area or qualitatively relates pressure and speed
common_misconceptions: fluid speed is independent of pipe diameter
Bernoulli principle means faster fluid has higher pressure

common_misclassifications.reason: both involve conservation laws; 8.4 applies to fluid flow
